When you call c.whois, a whois is a message sent to the server. It's an asynchronous operation for which the response may or may not come later. I've never used this feature before, but I suspect to catch the response, you need to define 'on_whois(self, c, e)' and parse out the message. If that doesn't work, then you'll have to dig into the IRC RFC and find out what message is sent in response to a whois query, and define 'on_<thatmessage>'.

Good luck, and please report back what you find.

I suspect there will need to be some escaping and unescaping, but it's not obvious to me without more investigation what that would be.

No problem. There is a way to tell your python to use encodings other than ascii for the implicit conversions that take place in 2.X:

```python
import sys
reload(sys)
sys.setdefaultencoding('utf-8')
```

with this enabled, `__str__` can successfully encode its return value to `str`. I've never actually seen anyone do this, but maybe this was the reason that the code didn't except on your machine.

The snippets by @ryanpdwyer are IMO the way to go here: In python2 the `__str__` method should encode its return value using some specified encoding - probably using from `locale.getpreferredencoding()`. If you don't feel this should be changed in pint, the `u"".format(quantity)` is the best python2/3 portable way to receive unicode strings.

I guess the issue is that ``get_function_perspective_globals`` inherently cannot be cached, because its result depends on the caller which is not explicitly part of the call's arguments.
``resolve_fw_decl`` optionally takes a custom globals argument ``globs`` where you can specify what it is supposed to consider global. Maybe you could cache the ``globs`` object. If ``resolve_fw_decl`` is called with ``globs`` != ``None``, it does not call slow ``get_function_perspective_globals``.

In general we can maybe introduce caching of ``resolve_fw_decl`` results. I think the forward references involved in a code base are usually limited...

Honestly, I'm not sure that the `ABCMeta` metaclass is doing very much. No tests fail on Python 3.x that pass on Python 2.x (there's an issue with `test_ipv6_resolution_udp` on Travis but that's true on all Python versions except Pypy).

The only thing we're doing with it is ensuring all the necessary abstract methods are implemented—but neither `StatsClientBase` nor `PipelineBase` are intended to be extended by userland code, they're purely internal. That means we can solve this multiple ways. I don't particularly want to add a dependency for this on either `six` or `future`. The fact that all the tests pass on 3.6 (at least when I run it locally) is kind of enough for me.

I'm going to pull out the metaclass and dependency on `abc`, and have the unimplemented methods `raise NotImplementedError()` instead. But in the meantime, I don't actually believe there's any externally-facing behavior that doesn't work on Py3 here. There are some internal guarantees that aren't being backstopped by the `ABCMeta` infrastructure—but they are being guaranteed by the test suite.

1、其实无论是select还是epoll、kqueue，其实都不是真正的异步io，只能称为io多路复用，他本身在读取数据还是和阻塞模式是一样的，区别只是我用用select或者epoll向系统注册了io读写事件监听器，等真正我们去读取数据的时候数据已经准备好了，所以不会产生阻塞，如果你不等待系统io事件直接去读数据，其实还是很阻塞同步io一样的，而等待系统io读写事件通知又需要我们主动发起并且是阻塞式的，真正的异步应该是我告诉你需要什么东西，你准备好通知我，所以这是阻塞的。

那么为什么又会说异步呢？其实是这样的，在真正的实际使用中，我们会给socket创建的时候会设置一个NONBLOCK标记，这样如果我们没有在系统io告知的时候去读取数据，那么会立刻返回一个io错误表示已经没有数据了，这样线程不会阻塞在io读取时间从而可以释放出cpu去接着做其他事情，比如接着监听是否还有其他io读写事件。
https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/master/tornado/iostream.py#L1094 你可看这。

之所以要这么设计，是因为在实际使用中，当io读取事件到达时我们并不知道有多少数据准备好了，但读取缓冲区是有限的，亦有可能在我们读取数据处理的过程中又有新的数据到达，现在都已经是万兆网了，所以我们并不能一次读取完所有数据，所以一般会循环读取多次知道io返回错误，从而告知我们可以继续处理其他io事件或者继续监听系统io。
https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/master/tornado/iostream.py#L680 具体过程你可以在这看到。

当然异步还有第二层含义是，当我们通过监听系统io读写事件到达时，可以立刻就用当前线程去处理io读写事件，比如tornado就是这么做的，也可以把这个事件一个队列里，之后再调度这个队列去处理io读写，调度的过程可以仍然由这个线程完成也可以由其他线程完成，比如java的Reactor，go语言的协程实现。
https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/master/tornado/ioloop.py#L921 具体过程你可以在这看到。

这两者的区别来说这两者并不大，cpu核心数总是有限的，而没有cpu核心同一时刻只能运行一个线程，如果在没有io阻塞产生的系统中，线程不会暂停始终占据cpu运行时间，那么已经是系统效率最大了，使用另外的线程调度io事件并不能加快系统运行效率，额外的线程还会导致更复杂的内存同步为题，所以一般第二种设计中，会使用一个线程监听select或epoll等待系统io事件，而用和cpu核心数相同的线程数处理io读写事件。

2、为什么一个线程可以既处理select监听系统io事件，handle_event，又可以发起注册handler，是因为所有需要做的事情都是以io事件来驱动的，select虽然会阻塞线程，但是现在同样也没有任何需要处理的事情，handle_event过程一般只是完成数据读取简单处理过程，所以这个过程会非常快，所以也不会影响其他事件的处理，而注册handler这个过程大部分情况会在handle_event的过程中发起，或者由外部的其他线程发起，所以虽然是个while死循环，但是仍然可以不停的处理各种事情。

假如在select还是其他普通事件没有处理完成，那么tornado会给select设置一个0的超时返回时间，那么select就不会再阻塞，此时并可以立刻接着处理其他事情。
假如在select阻塞的过程中需要处理超时事件，tornado的做法的会事先把超时按从小到大排序，选取小的超时时间作为select的超时等待返回时间，这样tornado就可以在超时事件到期的时候立刻开始处理。
https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/f5df1e4df7ca149342957f71bf4f98710f6cccdf/tornado/ioloop.py#L859 具体过程你可以在这看到。

假如在select的时候系统有其他任务想处理，比如响应系统退出事件，或者其他线程有io操作需要处理，那么tornado可以使用waker唤醒线程返回，具体做法是事先创建一个pipe或本地socket注册到系统中，需要唤醒线程时往里边写一个字符，select并会收到事件并返回，此时就可以接着处理其他事情了，从而达到唤醒线程的目的。
https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/3236f0704a2522a208d4d43afcecf2b8e6bfb538/tornado/platform/common.py#L27 具体过程你可以看这个waker的实现就知道了。

3、tornado只使用一个线程运行，除了上面所说cpu核心同一时刻只能运行一个线程，在没有io阻塞的情况下cpu已经是满负载了，以及使用单个线程可以不需要处理负载的内存同步过程，可以简化系统结构提供效率，同时tornado这样的以io事件驱动的系统一般面向的是比如推送、高并发web api、代理等io非常频繁非常多单cpu计算量很小的系统。

在这样的系统中，很多时候需要同时处理数万、数十万，甚至数百万的连接，如果以同步阻塞io的话，每个线程一个连接，但系统能创建的线程数是有上限的，系统的内存也是有限的，没创建一个线程只是栈就至少需要1MB，同时创建百万以上的线程还是很有困难的。

其次，系统再调度线程以及线程切换的过程中都是需要消耗资源的，在大量线程的系统中，线程调度和线程切换当会产生非常高的系统资源消耗，而使用单一线程处理所有连接事件将会大幅度提高系统性能。

tornado使用单一线程处理还有一个很重要的原因也是受python全局GIL锁的影响，统一时刻，只能有一个线程真实的在运行，所以在生产环境部署中，一般会创建和cpu核心数相同的进程来充分使用系统资源。

### 在tornado源码中：

ioloop.py 完成io事件循环，start函数就是io时间主循环过程了

iostream.py 完成io读写过程，_read_to_buffer就是核心取过程

这两个文件基本就是tornado的io核心过程了，platform下边还放了一些针对各平台的一些具体实现

Hi all,

I'm the owner of `PyCQA/pydocstyle` and I started working on replacing our underlying parsing engine. I explicitly want to use a 3rd party parser so that it won't be a part of pydocstyle. We currently have a crude parsing implementation which is very fragile and buggy.
Today I tried to replace our internal engine with `pylint`'s `astroid` engine, but I found that it didn't keep enough low-level information about the code (specifically, I'm interested in the exact formatting of docstrings, and not just the resulting string object).
I then tried using `redbaron` and found it much better in that I was able to easily access the raw docstring (although I had to "fish" for it myself - i.e., look for a string expression, instead of it parsing it automatically). However, I understand that `redbaron` has some performance issues, so that might be a deal breaker.

I'm not sure if I can commit to maintain a parsing library (my wife is expected to be giving birth any day now, so I'll have very little time), but I am definitely going to spend the time and replace `pydocstyle`'s parsing engine.

This is a little bit complicated.  I think probably has to do with how and when `gsis_names` get added into the metadata stored by `nflgame`.  

The cases where stats are "missing" are for related reasons.  Will take Odell Beckham as an example but similar problems will hold true for the others.

When the NFL added him, they were using `gsis_name` of "O.Beckham Jr.".  This appears in the file `players.json`:
```
    "00-0031235": {
        "birthdate": "11/5/1992",
        "college": "LSU",
        "first_name": "Odell",
        "full_name": "Odell Beckham",
        "gsis_id": "00-0031235",
        "gsis_name": "O.Beckham Jr.",
        "height": 71,
        "last_name": "Beckham",
        "number": 13,
        "position": "WR",
        "profile_id": 2543496,
        "profile_url": "http://www.nfl.com/player/odellbeckham/2543496/profile",
        "status": "ACT",
        "team": "NYG",
        "weight": 198,
        "years_pro": 3
    },
```
and can be accessed in `nflgame`:
```python
import nflgame
player = nflgame.find('Odell Beckham')[0]
print player, player.gsis_name
```

In an effort to not be rude to NFL.com, those thousands of players that are in `nflgame` aren't updated once they're in the system.  They're kind of frozen.  So, if the NFL changes what short name they call them, then `nflgame` doesn't ever know.  In the case of Beckham, his short name has changed multiple times back and forth throughout the years.  In a similar vein, when the NFL added Malcolm Mitchell & Ty Montgomery to their rosters, they didn't even assign them an `gsis_id`.  (See players "00-0032400" and "00-0032200" in the previously mentioned `players.json` file).

But even without those issues, in truth `gsis_name` isn't a very good "name" to use since it may or may not be unique to the player (think about, for example, Charles Johnson vs. Calvin Johnson vs. Chris Johnson gsis_name).  A much better - and most importantly, unique - reference is the `player_id`.

I won't change your code too much (though it's pretty redundant to loop over every single game for every single player)...

```

            print "rec:", game_player.receiving_rec,
            print "yds:", game_player.receiving_yds,
            print "tds:", game_player.receiving_tds
    print '-'*25
```

The first issue with all options showing up is because the filtering order isn't saved. So the second filter "thinks" the Portfolio column was filtered first (so all options show), then the "FilterProjStatus" column gets filtered second. Fixing this will take some rewriting of the filter widget, which I don't really have the time for at the moment, but I'll keep this on my to do list.

As for the second issue, the filter uses "and" as a key word to allow searching for content that matches both (or multiple) words: "1 and 2". The easiest fix would be to [change the key word](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-widget-filter.html#localization) to something else (just make sure your users know about the change ([demo](http://jsfiddle.net/Mottie/wb5xgkew/4/)):

```js
// change "and" filter to something else
$.tablesorter.language.and = "&&";
```

Hi @nikolas!

Have you seen [this uitheme widget demo](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-widget-bootstrap-theme.html)?

It really doesn't matter what element is used for the Bootstrap glyphicons, but if you use the "uitheme" widget, it sets the default icons to use as follows (copied from the javascript example in the above linked demo):

```js
// class name added to icon when column has ascending sort
iconSortAsc  : 'glyphicon glyphicon-chevron-up',
// class name added to icon when column has descending sort
iconSortDesc : 'glyphicon glyphicon-chevron-down',
```

These classes are applied to an `<i>` element which is added to the header when an `{icon}` pattern is added to the [`headerTemplate` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/index.html#headertemplate). If you don't like using the `<i>` element, simply modify the `headerTemplate` to add a different element which can be targeted by a custom theme definition.

So, while this pull request adds basic sort arrows to the bootstrap theme, it does not allow for adding glyphicons using the `<i>` element. Both will be present and may not be appealing.

----

Alternatively, if you don't use the uitheme widget, the plugin targets an element within the header with a class name of `tablesorter-icon` (modified by the [`cssIcon` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/index.html#cssicon)) to update the sort icon status using the values from the [`cssIconAsc`](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/index.html#cssiconasc) and [`cssIconDesc`](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/index.html#cssicondesc) options.

Let's separate this in two pieces:
The pattern and is_authenticated

We all are agree that is_authenticated doesn't work as expected (or unfortunated name at least)
I would prefer is_authenticated returns what is expected rather than change name/disapear

The pattern question, thought, is another discussion: any pattern has its place but misplacing it is dangerous and tend to confuse the reader of the code

For instance authenticate, it returns always 401 unauthorized so doesn't make any point to know the reason cause will be a security concern to inform the client of the particular problem (email/name doesn't exist, password failed, ...)

I understand that one tends to fit patterns everywhere to avoid heterogeneous code but a misplaced pattern is two problems: the misplacement will confuse the reader till they realise it is a patternitis issue and the poor solution that the pattern itself introduces (that you call optimization code but for me is making things with common sense)

It's actually worse than that, the `objectType` and `propertyList` properties are included in the value of the `propertyList` and they shouldn't be:

> [The Property_List] read-only property is a BACnetARRAY of property identifiers, one property identifier for each property that exists within the object. The Object_Name, Object_Type, Object_Identifier, and Property_List properties are not included in the list.

One of the design concepts of BACpypes object instances is that unless you give it a property value in the kwargs, or that it gets one from the class definition, the property doesn't get a value:
```
>>> from bacpypes.object import Object
>>> o = Object()
>>> [k for k, v in o._values.items() if v is not None]
[]
```
So by that reasoning, this is wrong because the only thing provided by the class definition is the `objectType` and the `propertyList` shouldn't be there:
```
>>> from bacpypes.object import DeviceObject
>>> do = DeviceObject()
>>> [k for k, v in do._values.items() if v is not None]
['objectType', 'propertyList']
```
But when I provide the value as a kwarg I want it to be there and be exactly what I told it, without magic:
```
>>> from bacpypes.constructeddata import ArrayOf
>>> from bacpypes.object import PropertyIdentifier
>>> 
>>> do = DeviceObject(propertyList=ArrayOf(PropertyIdentifier)([]))
>>> do._values['propertyList'].value
[0]
```
With a `LocalDeviceObject` providing some additional property values by default and some automatic handling of those properties, like `localDate` and `localTime`, then it makes sense that the `propertyList` would be like the `objectList` where it is created and magic:
```
>>> from bacpypes.service.device import LocalDeviceObject
>>> ldo = LocalDeviceObject()
>>> [k for k, v in ldo._values.items() if v is not None]
['numberOfApduRetries', 'maxSegmentsAccepted', 'maxApduLengthAccepted', 'objectType', 'apduTimeout', 'apduSegmentTimeout', 'segmentationSupported', 'vendorIdentifier', 'objectList', 'propertyList']
>>> ldo._values['propertyList'].value
[10, 'propertyList', 'numberOfApduRetries', 'maxSegmentsAccepted', 'apduTimeout', 'apduSegmentTimeout', 'segmentationSupported', 'vendorIdentifier', 'maxApduLengthAccepted', 'objectType', 'objectList']
```
And your patch helps a little because it does appear, but only when the `objectList` is not provided, which seems wrong.  If the `objectList` is supposed to be magic when the kwarg is not provided, then maybe the `propertyList` should act the same way - _or_ - both the `objectList` and `propertyList` are going to be like `localDate` and `localTime` where they will always be magic and init values cannot be provided.

In this branch I removed the `propertyList` management from the `Object` class and moved it to a new `LocalObject` class, then stuffed it in the inheritance chain of the `LocalDeviceObject`.  I also "promoted" the `objectList` property to be something managed by the `LocalDeviceObject`.  In order for the `objectList` to be created with the correct initial value (a single element array containing the object identifier of the device object) then the `objectIdentifier` property is required.

This is a pretty significant change in behavior that might break backward compatibility if there are applications that are relying on `propertyList` to be automatically handled for generic objects like analog values.  The applications can do this to get the behavior back:
```
class LocalAnalogValueObject(LocalObject, AnalogValueObject):
    pass
```
In the grand scheme of things I think this is a better separation of responsibility.

From looking at it briefly, None is meant to be "not specified" and numbers are meant to specify intended behavior in a case of null != 0. Particularly for the three lines above, that doesn't make sense: we'll only be setting `wantprefixlen = None` if it's already None.

That said, I'll look into it in more detail and see about setting a test case and static variable names to be less confusing (PREFIXLEN = 1, NETMASK = 2, etc.)

Hi,

It would definitely be handy if this was built-in. It's a really common use-case. I can imagine two features out of randomness:

0. Execute the job with a delay of ±x seconds. This allows randomizing execution time. It would prevent all jobs to be trigger at the exact same time and has obvious benefits. It also allows a weak round-robin between nodes when the first node that executes the job is the one that wins.
0. Execute the job with a given probability. You use a random variable to decide whether the job should execute or not. This is especially useful for short `IntervalTrigger` because it's a way to strengthen the round-robin.

It is true that it's possible to subclass existing triggers to achieve this. I've been using this piece of code for a while and it works fine (also have some tests for it):
```python
class FuzzyIntervalTrigger(IntervalTrigger):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        FuzzyTrigger allows to schedule jobs that will execute at fuzzy intervals.
        Jobs will fire every interval ± max_fuzz, with a given probability.

        e.g. seconds = 300, max_fuzz = 30, probability = .5 means that one time out of two, the job will fire at
        interval ± max_fuzz.

        :param max_fuzz: The maximum uncertainty on the next fire. Task will neither trigger before interval - max_fuzz,
        nor after interval + max_fuzz (Default: 0)
        :type max_fuzz: float|int
        :param probability: The probability with which the task should execute. 1 means the task will always execute,
        .5 means the task will execute one time out of two. Too small values will affect performances. 0 will loop
        indefinitely. (Default: 1.)
        :type probability: float|int
        :param args: IntervalTrigger args
        :param kwargs: IntervalTrigger kwargs
        """
        self.__max_fuzz = kwargs.pop('max_fuzz', 0)
        self.__probability = kwargs.pop('probability', 1.)

        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def get_next_fire_time(self, previous_fire_time, now):
        SAFEGUARD = 10000
        for _ in range(0, SAFEGUARD):
            next_fire_time = super().get_next_fire_time(previous_fire_time, now)
            if next_fire_time is None:
                return None
            next_fire_time += datetime.timedelta(seconds=random.uniform(self.__max_fuzz * -1, self.__max_fuzz))
            if next_fire_time < now:
                next_fire_time = now
            if random.random() < self.__probability:
                return next_fire_time
            now = previous_fire_time or now
            previous_fire_time = next_fire_time
        raise RuntimeError('Infinite loop detected.')
```

**BUT** it would be great if this was more deeply integrated with APScheduler, allowing this type of API:
```python
IntervalTrigger(seconds=5, probability=.5, fuzzyness=5)
CronTrigger(hour=22, fuzzyness=600)
``` 

I'm keen on contributing for this. Just let me know how if the wontfix could be reconsidered and how you would see the API.

To run forever, you should use 
`bokeh_server = False` option
and when you define a device, add the argument `poll=0` This will deactivate polling from every points in the device.

You could also use a new feature (in the develop branch) that allow to specify the point list when defining a device. This would allow you to keep the polling feature active while restricting the point list.

Here is an extract from the doc : 

```
**Object List**

By default, BAC0 will read the object list from the controller and define every
points found inside the device as points. This behaviour may not be optimal in
all use cases. BAC0 allows you to provide a custom object list when creating the
device.

To do so, use this syntax::    

    # Define your own list
    my_obj_list = [('file', 1),
                 ('analogInput', 2),
                 ('analogInput', 3),
                 ('analogInput', 5),
                 ('analogInput', 4),
                 ('analogInput', 0),
                 ('analogInput', 1)]
    
    # Provide it as an argument               
    fx = BAC0.device('2:5',5,bacnet, object_list = my_obj_list)
```

Hi @imaia,

This behaviour is expected.
The idea of `django-dirty-fields` is to compare in-memory field values and database ones for a given object.

In the first example, your object is only in memory and has no database record for it, so `django-dirty-fields` considers that he is dirty.
In the second example, you just saved the model in the database, so your in-memory object and your database record are identical, so your model is not "dirty".

The main idea of `django-dirty-fields` is to know if it is worth to save an object in the database. If this object has not changed since its last saving, we could economise some database queries.

```
model = Model.objects.create(field=x)
assert model.is_dirty() == False

model.field = x

# This save will generate a database query that is useless, as field value is already x
model.save()  

# To avoid this useless database query, you could have done:
if model.is_dirty():
    model.save()

# OR
model.save_dirty_fields()
```

Our project uses Marshmallow to define the models, so this integration is a key feature for us.

I checked flask-apispec a while ago. It didn't have all the features we required, and it didn't seem to show much [re|]activity. I wasn't comfortable with it because I found it hard understanding the code, so I couldn't modify it to fit my needs (doesn't mean it is _that_ twisted, could be me being noobish on that one).

We ended up crafting an equivalent of our own: [flask-rest-api](https://github.com/Nobatek/flask-rest-api). It is inspired by flask-restplus and flask-apispec and relies on apispec and webargs. It also deals with etag and pagination. There is no docs yet and the direct test coverage is poor, although it is tested through our project. Needless to say it is by no means stable, as we introduce breaking changes as we go. There are also a few issues in the generated docs.

AFAIK, flask-restplus is the most stable and featured alternative. I'd probably be using it if our project was not so Marshmallow-based.

ok, actually a small but interesting issue. It is related to the use of the `\LaTeX` macro. In order to have it interpreted by mathjax, we have to enclose it between dollars. It is converted as is when converting to latex. But this macro is a text mode macro, and thus latex complains that it is not uses as it should (but we must enclose it between $ in order to have it interpreted in the livenotebook or in html)...  
Thus for the moment I have introduced a simple substitution in the notebook --> latex conversion. 

More importantly, looking at the other errors, I realized that the processing of comments (any line beginning by a % is ignored) was not always correct. This was because the latex text is reflowed sometimes in a strange way during the markdown to latex conversion. I have introduced and pushed a fix for that. Anyway, one should have a look at the log file since the conversion may not be perfect (actually the text resulting for standard notebook -> latex conversion is also not always perfect)

@ricellis, @rhyshort, @emlaver, 

What are your thoughts on tracking the session start time as a client attribute and basing the refresh of the cookie auth on the difference between the time of a request and the session start time?  

Prior to honoring the request we would perform this check and if the difference is greater than a certain amount (lets say 23 hours) then we would perform the POST to the _session endpoint to refresh the cookie auth before proceeding to handle the original request.  This could all be managed via a kwarg passed in when constructing the client so the default behavior can remain the same as it is now.  This would be a really simple solution since all of the classes that require an `r_session` currently already return it via a function with the `@property` decorator.  So the necessary code to do this comparison already has a convenient home.  I think the overhead would be relatively small to perform the time comparison prior to each request and a user of the library would have to expressly ask for this behavior via the additional keyword argument so they could/would be aware that they are asking for additional "reconnect" functionality that has some overhead.

Let me know your thoughts.

This overload new meaning into the term `backfill`. Let's clarify some of the semantics here.

**backfill**: "a manually triggered CLI process to run or re-run task instances in the past"
**scheduler catchup** (new term): when the scheduler has task instances to run in the past. This assumes a range of dates between tasks' `start_date` and the current date, and active DagRuns in between.

I'd suggest renaming `DAG.backfill` to `allow_scheduler_catchup`.

Well. The luigi philosophy is that "workers knows better than the scheduler", once the user understands that this should feel natural.

Most of my crons only schedule recent jobs, with larger backfills scheduled less often to make sure we're not missing anything. The larger backfills would end up scheduling lots of older disabled jobs that would run down the timer before being rescheduled. This would also be a potential problem with large one-off runs that incidentally include disabled jobs. This could be fixed by setting the disable to a very long time when disabled by the scheduler.

This would also cause problems with unsetting disabled in the code. Right now if you set a job as disabled and later re-enable it, the central planner will see that it was disabled by scheduling and allow scheduling to re-enable it. With your solution, the job will now also have to be re-enabled by the user or wait several hours/days/whatever until the disable timer ran out. This problem becomes worse if we set scheduled disabled jobs to have a very long disable timer.

The other issue I'd have with this is manual re-enabling of automatically disabled jobs. I tend to have many jobs scheduled as disabled and one or two occasionally get automatically disabled. It's easy to find and re-enable the automatically disabled ones in the scheduler because they're the ones that have a re-enable button. It doesn't make much sense to re-enable a job that's regularly getting scheduled as disabled. For this reason, I've actually considered splitting these two kinds of disables into two different statuses.

@slovit How are you currently timing this?

If your are timing it by running the snippet you provided (including iteration of the objects), your results will be misleading for a couple of reasons:

1) Whenever a collection object is being used and the underlying operation is paginatable (such as ``s3_objects = bucket.objects.filter(Prefix=key_prefix)``), boto3 will make as many requests as needed to collect all of the results. So my guess is that when you are iterating over the objects from the collection, ``boto3`` is making multiple requests all that take less than one second to connect and read. Therefore, you will not got any timeouts.

2) If there is a timeout, boto3 will automatically do retries with exponential backoff. So if it timed out and retried 5 times using a timeout of 1 second, the total time waited will be longer than 5 seconds because with each retry boto3 will sleep exponentially longer before making another request.

The create operation is asynchronous, it will always return immediately. You can use [`describe_delivery_stream`](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/firehose.html#Firehose.Client.describe_delivery_stream) to check the status. Are you certain that it is going to `ACTIVE`? My hunch is that your splunk configuration isn't giving kinesis enough permissions to work.

If you still have more questions, I would recommend asking on the [service forum](https://forums.aws.amazon.com/forum.jspa?forumID=169) or on [stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/aws-kinesis-firehose) to engage people with more service experience.

Please see the sample at https://github.com/vmware/pyvmomi-community-samples/blob/master/samples/export_vm.py

A key thing to note is that ExportVm does not return a task. It returns an HttpNfcLease. The lease gives you details for downloading the VM. And during that download, you need to continue to issue progress updates else the server will time out the lease (thinking that you abandoned it).

As you can see from the sample, it is a non-trivial process for exporting a VM (but the sample is a good one to base off of).

Even though I am acknowledging the issue, I wonder whether it is worth fixing. Wouldn't the required information be conveyed if one would the following:

```python
# this would only work if revA is a full 40 character hexadecimal SHA1
revA in repo.merge_base(revA, revB)
```

A quick check of the underlying showed that the work done under the hood is comparable: The `--is-ancestor` flag ends up calling [`in_merge_bases_many(...)`](https://github.com/git/git/blob/master/commit.c#L1025), whereas the standard-path will end up calling [`get_merge_bases_many_dirty(...)`](https://github.com/git/git/blob/master/commit.c#L992) - both of which seem to do the majority of the work in [`paint_down_to_common`](https://github.com/git/git/blob/master/commit.c#L778). The standard-path does more processing, admittedly, still I'd be inclined to believe that both invocations spend more time doing IO than anything else.

The reason why I am hesitant to implement the proposed solution is that it would break the existing API in a stable release. Additionally I don't believe altering the return *type* of any routine based on its arguments is a good thing.

A proposed workaround with exact semantics is to make the git call directly, possibly through a utility function:

```python
def is_ancestor(repo, a, b):
    try:
        repo.git.merge_base(a, b, is_ancestor=True)
        return True
    except GitCommandError as err:
        if err.status == 1:
            return False
        raise
```

Please let me know what you think.

The choice of Python versions in #64 was based on `.travis.yml`. In the past I used to not include Python versions. Due to errors caused by dependencies (not here---in other packages), I started using Python versions, including both major and minor version numbers.

An example where the error was convoluted to uncover is (the commit message there contains more details):

https://github.com/tulip-control/tulip-control/commit/16d82b4fe18e144e0d342174956a38d3be32fcae

which led to removing the specifier for Python 3.3 from the classifiers of `matplotlib`, because Python 3.4 or higher was required by `matplotlib`, from the Python 3 series (https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/9085).

Since then, I have been first adding specific Python versions to the test environments on Travis CI (e.g., [this commit](https://github.com/tulip-control/tulip-control/commit/88e0574223319499b27061f3f688482a5aa79cb8)), and after the tests pass, declare support via Trove classifiers (e.g., [this commit](https://github.com/tulip-control/tulip-control/commit/b91fa3ff91d9dc4fd84981ff4d356c5a49f2eb91)).

So I have been using Trove classifiers for Python versions as the specification of the package, so that the information be visible on PyPI. Even if contiguous Python versions are supported, explicitly indicating the supported Python versions emphasizes that they are supported (when absent one could be unclear whether the Python version specifiers have been omitted or not considered at all).

As commented [above](https://github.com/sagemath/cysignals/pull/65#issuecomment-357182558), the `cysignals` package is mostly Python independent. I did not know whether that is the case, so I used the information from `.travis.yml`. (Also, `cysignals` requires Cython, and Cython lists specific Python versions [in its `setup.py`](https://github.com/cython/cython/blob/634a86f26c2da8fe37ee27d49c4d5be410376f59/setup.py#L264-L271), so those specifiers may be relevant here.)

Indeed, the need for updating the Python version classifiers increases logistics, so if the versions make little difference, then omitting them may simplify things. On the other hand, new Python versions will be added to `.travis.yml` as they become supported, so those commits could also add them to the classifiers, thus declaring support to `pip` and other packages.

Related to #1799 and recent discussion on documentation of community processes.

Django has an excellent security policy that can be used as inspiration:
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.7/internals/security/

I think the most important to document in a security policy is:
- How to report security issues.
- How we deal with a reported security issue (i.e. what is our workflow).

Currently we have unwritten processes but it would be good to get them down on paper. Also, I think we can wait with technical implementation details such as if we sign/encrypt messages, until the workflow is fully defined and documented.

The code looks fine in principle, but I have some reservations.

For a start I'm not sure this solution is really justified by the original motivation about well defined recipe versions. I don't disagree that versioned recipes is at least a small problem, but in practice it's never been an issue, and most of the examples in the original report (#1115) target master branches for a reason. That raises the question of what this PR adds, and I guess the answer is that the ability to easily distribute recipes via pip is a nice thing on its own, regardless of whether it really helps with recipe versioning.

However, I'm concerned that this may introduce recipe fragmentation that causes more problems than it solves. There are examples in the recipes you have already published: your fixed numpy recipe is great, but why not just do a PR with these fixes for p4a itself? Since the pip recipes override local p4a ones, anyone who has installed p4a-numpy won't get the benefit of new updates to p4a unless p4a-numpy is itself kept up to date with them. The same is true of e.g. your p4a-crystax package, which overrides the builtin python3crystax but doesn't actually change it. I'd be very happy to have a python2crystax in p4a master instead, would you be interested in adding it there?

For what it's worth, there's actually already a way to use local recipes in p4a builds without cloning p4a, using the `--local-recipes` argument (defaulting to a `p4a-recipes` folder in the current directory). Do you know about that? I guessed that you did, but actually I realise it isn't really documented. That's my mistake, I'll add some documentation about it shortly.

Yeah, I think statsmodels has similar trouble with their categorical models. Only question is what the interface to this should look like. What do you want to be able to request from patsy, and what kind of output should patsy support? Array of strings? Array of ints corresponding to the categories? (Annoyingly we still don't have a standard way to represent categorical data in numpy, so I guess we'll have to do something ad hoc...) When you process a formula, do you know ahead of time whether you want the response variable to be categorical, or do there exist models that want to do one thing if `y` is categorical and a different-but-equally-valid thing if `y` is numerical?

R's way of handling this is that whatever is on the left-hand side of formulas gets treated as R code rather than formula code, so in `x + y ~ z + w`, the first `+` does addition and the second `+` concatenates columns, which is rather confusing. And they have the luxury of having a single standard way to represent categorical data, so it's reasonable for the formula system to just get out of the way and let the end-user and the underlying model talk to each other directly. Not very helpful for us, unfortunately.

Decompression speed is about right.  Look at the benchmark I linked to.  The results table can be sorted by decompression speed; just click on the "Decompression Speed" header of the results table.

The problem is that this is a huge outlier for brotli's compression speed.  It's so slow that it basically amounts to a DoS vulnerability.  It takes an hour to compress an 8.1 MiB file (on a Xeon E3-1225 v3), which is 5.62 KiB/s.  The next slowest file (ooffice, which is a 5.87 MiB DLL from OpenOffice.org 1.01) is 59.9 KiB/s.

I think a lot of the compression speeds are pretty bad when you look at Brotli's competition (LZHAM is probably the closest competitor—it offers pretty similar ratios and decompression speed but much more stable, and generally faster compression).  But 5.62 KiB/s is just unacceptable.

@CyberDem0n I think you are correct in that it could also be fixed by using `backup_strings[].split('\t')`. I opted to use `csv.DictReader` because of the circumstances around it.

Previously:

- Split output into rows, assert # rows is `2`
- Split lines into columns, assert # columns are `7` in order to verify that parsing was done correctly.
- Convert rows of columns to dict using the first row's columns as keys and the second rows' columns as values

`csv.DictReader` does the same thing, and I suspect that it does it in a more robust way.

The benefits over just changing `.split('\t'):

- When wal-e decides to add more columns to the output, `csv.DictReader` won't make `should_use_s3[...]` fail silently.
- If wal-e would include the `\t` character in a column value it won't break our parsing of backups, additionally it will (should) be escaped on wal-e's end, and will be unescaped on our end.

```python
from algoliasearch_django import AlgoliaIndex
from algoliasearch_django.decorators import register 

from .models import TestSearch

@register(TestSearch)
```

Hello,  Technically speaking, your comments are clearly incorrect. See
below.  I think you would do better to try to compare and contrast the
strengths and weaknesses of different technical approaches than to make
false claims about one approach being the only way to go.

On 3/19/18 1:44 AM, Matthias König wrote:
> Hi all,
>
> I am a very strong supporter of a github based approach using structured
> text files (one can still have a database backend working in sync with the
> text files to get the best of both worlds).
> This is in my opinion the only way to get a community curated model and
> community curation on models.
The ONLY way?  Clearly that statement is incorrect.  If a model is stored in
a database, multiple users can connect to that database to update it.  
We have
had such a multi-user database update situation working for approximately 20
years, such as using our EcoCyc database.  Obviously this is not even new
technology.
> Otherwise it is very complicated to get changes/fixes into models. See for
> instance RECON3D where I found some errors but there is no clear platform
> for reporting this issues or even fixing this.
> With a text based github approach this fixes could be immediatly available
> to the community via a pull request
>
> I would love to fix things like the following reported issues in RECON3D
> https://github.com/SBRG/bigg_models/issues/291
> https://github.com/SBRG/bigg_models/issues/290
> https://github.com/SBRG/bigg_models/issues/287
>
> After more than 20 days of reporting the first issue of a incorrect 
> gene in
> RECON3D these are still not fixed in the underlying database
> http://vmh.uni.lu/#reaction/HDCA24Gtr
> Whereas in text file via pull request everybody could be immediatly 
> working
> with an updated Recon v3.02.
I'm sorry to hear that RECON3D has not implemented your suggested fixes.
That is irrelevant to the question of whether a git-based approach is 
the only
approach.
>
> The only way to get the community to work together and use the curation
> potential of all scientist working with models is a fully open easy to
> update format.
> Github with structured text file is an established and working approach.
Yes, it is an established and working approach, but to say it is the only
approach is incorrect.  There are other established and working approaches,
like a database approach.

You speak here of the need for "a fully open easy to update format".
The XML-based formats used by many model developers are not easy
to update and are not at all git-friendly.  That is, if two developers 
end up
committing conflicting changes, deciphering and resolving the git conflict
messages will be a nightmare; probably it will be beyond the capabilities of
many model builders to understand these messages.  Therefore one could 
argue that
the approach you propose is not workable if resolving conflicts becomes
impossible because you are mis-using source-code control systems for
complex file formats they were not designed to work with.
>
> I really hope the community is working into this direction instead of
> hiding information in in-house databases.
It is strange that you use the phrases "hiding information" and "in-house
databases".  Yes, to update the shared database, a user would have to
be granted access to the database.  Just as to update a shared git 
repository,
a user has to be granted access to the repository.  So one could just as 
well
accuse a git-based project of "hiding information in a private repository".

Windows uses [64 bit signed integers to represent time in number of 100-nanonsecond chunks since the year 1601](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms724290), which results in the latest possible representable time being 100*(2^63-1) Nanoseconds since epoch, which is the year 30828.

The `0.20.0.dev0` shows you've installed from source, since [0.19.0][1] is the latest version on PyPI.

I recommend the following:

1. Uninstall these problematic packages:

    ```
    sudo --set-home pip uninstall google-cloud protobuf
    ```

1. Install `google-cloud` from PyPI rather than from source:

    ```
    sudo --set-home pip install google-cloud
    ```

----

Also, Mac OS X is essentially impossible to get a valid Python install because they have packages that you can't edit or delete (like `six`) that will be out of date.

I **highly** recommend you exclusively use `virtualenv`s on a Mac or just use a non-default Python like Anaconda or Homebrew.

```python
128   @property

```

The class that hosts these methods exists in `pex/resolver_options.py` in the `ResolverOptionsBuilder` class. Maybe I'll submit a PR, but I've already spent enough time on this, to be continued :).

@kyleknap I can see where you're coming from about the environment variables thing, but I'm not sure if it's entirely relevant in the current state of variable support of the shell. Upon entering the `aws-shell` we copy the parent process environment and have an internal copy that we pass to sub-processes. Once this copy is made it can no longer be manipulated by the user within the shell. We do not support `export` or `source` currently. If this was a CLI feature I would totally agree, but I'm not sure if the same logic applies to the shell's internal environment variables. This is a shell feature. You are setting the profile for the shell, and if you were to do that in bash or zsh you would simply export the profile. This is an alias for that behavior in our shell.

I really don't think the behavior of `.profile` should be to append `--profile <profile>` to cli commands. This would require us to check every cli command before it's executed for the presence of a profile and only in the case that it's not passed should it be appended. There may be other situations where simply adding `cmd + '  --profile {profile}'` will not be sufficient. I also don't think the command the user types and the command that is executed should ever differ. This will complicate the command history and the `.edit` functionality.

1. I also prefer dynamic fixtures over static fixtures, as suggested by the [Django docs on testing](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/topics/testing/tools/#fixture-loading), mainly due to the high maintenance cost static fixtures incur.
1. I'm not sure how static fixtures can be generated from callables. Would be interesting to know.
1. Following PEP 20's idea of _"there should be one obvious way to do it"_, we should (at least) recommend one way over the other, similar to _"tests are more readable and it’s more maintainable to create objects using the ORM"_ in Django's testing docs. -- In the end, loading fixtures with annotations will result in the "creation" of the `fixtures` attribute on `context`. So, modifying `context.fixtures` would be "manipulating" the object part of the process directly, we could argue.

Basically, you need to add some metadata about the tokens that keeps track of this information.  If you're using spacy as your tokenizer, spacy keeps track of this, and we use it when [making `Instances` for reading comprehension](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/cbeef924a448cb9f8dfb7b5832d5d5f98d8262fc/allennlp/data/dataset_readers/reading_comprehension/util.py#L186-L192).

For adding this to an SRL model, you have to change some code.  You need the `Instances` to have the character offsets that you want (you could add this to the [dataset reader](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/cbeef924a448cb9f8dfb7b5832d5d5f98d8262fc/allennlp/data/dataset_readers/semantic_role_labeling.py#L109-L110), or by [hacking the `Instances` in the predictor](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/cbeef924a448cb9f8dfb7b5832d5d5f98d8262fc/allennlp/predictors/semantic_role_labeler.py#L101-L102)), you need the model to [pass through the metadata field in `forward`](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/cbeef924a448cb9f8dfb7b5832d5d5f98d8262fc/allennlp/models/semantic_role_labeler.py#L153-L155), and you need the predictor to format the information how you want it in [`predict_json`](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/cbeef924a448cb9f8dfb7b5832d5d5f98d8262fc/allennlp/predictors/semantic_role_labeler.py#L207-L211).

This is a bit of an annoying process just to get some additional metadata into some JSON output.  We had a different API earlier that would have made this a little bit easier, because you could just add some stuff directly to the predictor, but that way had its own problems.

As far as `.flat` goes, there's totally nothing wrong with the user using `.flat` in his or her own work -- I use it all the time (though `.recurse()` has passed it in terms of frequency).  It is that individual methods in `.stream()` should not, generally, be working with `.flat`.  

Here's why: say there's a method called "gCounter(self)" that counts the number of (pitch) G's in a Stream.  There's two possible interpretations for what this might mean: count all the Gs in notes/chords in the stream, or count all the Gs in the notes/chords in the stream and any substreams (i.e., `.flat`).  The second one might sound more useful, so it might make sense to have `gCounter()` begin with something like `sf = self.flat`.  But what would happen if you wanted the former (no-substream) interpretation?  Then there's no way to do it.  If, however, `gCounter()` worked only on the stream and no substreams, then it would be easy to do either version: call `self.gCounter()` to get the no-substream version, and `self.flat.gCounter()` to get the substream-aware version.

If a method needs to do something more sophisticated (such as reaching into substreams but still be passed the hierarchical version of the stream), like I think `.secondsMap()` does, then it should have a `{recurse=True}` configurable option, or something like that.  I might get to patching this soon, but I'll make sure that the interface to `.secondsMap` doesn't change without letting you know. :-)

@bd4 I agree that it's hairy (really hairy), but this is about the best that we've got to date.
Defining a custom decorator which attaches multiple options is basically the standard way of doing this in `click`.

You can handle options as non-decorator objects, but when it comes time to attach them you'll probably end up stuffing them back inside a decorator anyway.
I'm not sure that there's a way of polishing this turd any further than I have -- if options are attached to functions, then they need to be wrapped in a decorator to be syntactically clean.

---

A lot of it is working around the decorator syntax in general though, and is the best way of doing this particular pattern that I've seen.

The issue is that I find this ugly and confusing (you may disagree):
```python
@common_options()
def funcA(...):
    ...
```

I would much rather write
```python
@common_options
def funcB(...):
    ...
```

Requiring those extra parens by not accepting a function directly to the decorator results in confusing stacktraces when someone (who doesn't know what they are and why they're required) messes up and writes the latter form. It's a stacktrace from the decorator application, not function invocation, and likely to confuse.
It also seems fussy to require empty parens to a decorator -- as a caller, I either want to be giving it special arguments or not.

Either you need empty parens whenever you use the base decorator, which looks odd and confusing, or you specially handle the case of a single positional argument.
I'd rather put the ugly on the inside than the outside, though i see the argument against all of this fanciness as well.

(Hey, at least I didn't write a decorator decorator to handle this stuff! :stuck_out_tongue_winking_eye: )

I agree that it needs good/thorough comments, but I thought that what I had was sufficient.

This has conflicts now that #162 is merged. I also think there are some (unfortunate) reasons why you'll need to make some logical tweaks around the handling of the tokens you get back.

IIRC, Auth is upgrading all requests to include OIDC scopes, 'profile', 'email', 'openid', even if they aren't requested.
That means that even if someone passes `scopes='...transfer.api...'`, they'll get a two-token response. And, though I don't think this is a public promise, the auth.globus.org token will be the "top level" one.

These behaviors are part of the rationale for #162. There are some scenarios (e.g. refreshing a refresh token) when only one token _is_ returned. The results are inconsistent and confusing without internal knowledge of Auth, so the only safe thing to do is to use the `by_resource_server` representation and assume that there might be multiple tokens in every response.

> One design question I ran into was how to handle scopes that cross multiple resource servers?
Right now I say its required that all scopes be for the same resource server and raise a GlobusError when we get back other_tokens. But this prevents using the DEFAULT_REQUESTED_SCOPES and requires a decent amount of oauth2 knowledge for using the authorizer.

Others may differ, but I think this is the right basic approach. However, given that your scope set may be changed by the server, the error needs to be "when the provided scopes span multiple tokens in the response". It's a more laborious check than just `other_tokens` being nonempty.

The fact that it requires some more knowledge to use is okay. I think trying to make it too automagic may ultimately hurt the user in this case -- they'll need to gain some basic familiarity at some point.

> Require a resource_server parameter along with the scopes, so we can use .by_resource_server like we suggest our users do. Could make this optional and default to auth or transfer.

I'd be okay with this, but I don't think it's as good as deducing the RS from the scopes. If someone is passing scopes to this authorizer which span multiple resource servers, they've probably misunderstood something. I don't know that helping them select back into that data will actually help, and it will be an irritant for folks who _do_ know what they're doing.

> Unless of course its possible to have more than one Bearer token in the Authorization header, in which case I can just use all of the tokens, but I don't think the oauth2 spec allows that? (and if so could someone point me to the spec for those headers?)

It is not possible for spec-compliant servers. The whole idea of having multiple tokens is to not let one server see credentials intended for other resource servers -- doing this would violate the spirit of multiple tokens.

If you really want the spec, [RFC 6749](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749) is one of my _least favorite_ documents.
You should also feel free to ask the Auth devs questions like this: Auth is an opinionated OAuth2 service, so many things which are left discretionary or open by the spec are actually subject to choices that they've made.

> Also, I would like to remove the option to pass an initial access token to these authorizers, unless there is a good reason to keep it that I'm missing? My issue is that if a user passes a valid initial access token and an invalid refresh token/confidential client, errors will only come up when the authorizer tries to get a new access token, which feels like it could get complaints of "why did it suddenly stop working".

I don't mind dropping it for the `ClientCredentialsAuthorizer`, but it should stay for the `RefreshTokenAuthorizer`. It makes more sense for the latter than the former.

When you request refresh tokens, you get access tokens back alongside them. If the user can't pass in the initial access token, we're forcing an extra API round-trip, and making Auth mint an extra token.
Personally, I don't consider this a big deal, but we discussed it when we first added `RefreshTokenAuthorizer`s, and the consensus was that it was important to support and recommend a behavior which is the preferred OAuth2 client behavior.

That said, there isn't the same constraint on `ClientCredentialsAuthorizer`. There's no reason I can think of to force someone to do a client credentials grant by hand just to pass the result into the Authorizer.

Regarding scipy and in general: conda packages being 2.5x larger than wheels seems to be a matter of sub-optimal compile flags in `conda-build`. Those must be dragged in implicitly, via different compiler/OS selection, because they're not in build recipes and such flags are very hard to change without monkeypatching `numpy.distutils`. This should be the easiest one to figure out and fix.

Cython is fairly heavily used in both scipy and pandas, and indeed results in quite large binaries. There's a lot of boilerplate per .so, so combining multiple functions in a single .pyx helps. And you have to be very careful with Tempita templating to support multiple dtypes, combinatorial explosion happens quickly. Example: the first implementation of a Cythonized`scipy.ndimage.label` (a single simple function) was 4 MB, now it's still 70 kb. There's still a note on needing to investigate how much Cython usage we can tolerate on the scipy roadmap [here](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/roadmap.html#other).

> Anecdotally I've noticed that avoiding and then installing scipy results in a 400MB increase, so presumably there are a number of dependency packages that were brought on.

This is not the case, scipy only depends on numpy. I suspect you've experienced a version selection issue where MKL gets dragged back in (priority issues between `default` and `conda-forge` with such kind of effects used to be common, not sure if that's still the case).

I would definitely say that the fact build 1 of `gdal` has _completely_ relaxed the version requirements on `libgdal` is a bug. But that's a bug in the metadata, not in conda, and we should file an issue over on the anaconda side.

What's happening is that when it attempts to do the "close" action, there are no indices that need to be closed that match the filters provided, hence the `No actionable items in the list` message.

It may be that this is the case, that they're already closed.  This would result in an empty list of indices.  This raises an exception, internally, because you can't close indices if there aren't any in the list.  Curator provides a way to ignore an empty list condition.  For example, you are setting up Curator to close indices older than 30 days, but you only have 10 days of indices right now.  This would result in an empty list condition, but it is expected.  Anytime where an empty list is a non-fatal exception, you can skip the current action and to move on to the next action in the configuration by setting `ignore_empty_list: True` in the `options` section.

If you switch to log in DEBUG mode, you'll have a much more complete list of what is going on.  If you want me to help continue troubleshooting this, please attach a debug log (sanitize it of sensitive information if needed).

Ideally we'd have 1 upstream tarball for each, the SDK and the CLI such that we can create

python-azure-sdk-x.y.z and azure-cli.a.b.c packages with azure-cli.a.b.c depending on python-azure-sdk-x.y.z

Anyway, I understand, as does probably everyone else interested in this topic, that there are tradeoffs either way and going with a development model of individual components is just as valid a choice as going with a development model that keeps everything together. However, with the chosen model of many components people down stream (packagers or direct users) still need to have some moment in time every now and then where all the pieces fit together. Based on the finding of @glaubitz this point in time is incredibly difficult to determine.

So somehow a mechanism should exist that allows us to pull what would be considered a consistent SDK. If the answer to that is "whatever is on pypi" then that's OK, and maybe we just have to clean up a few things that @glaubitz ran across on pypi and then we are good to go.

I'm actually don't follow the topic you're fighting for (:
Trying to summarize for myself:
1. You made a request via `resp = await session.post()`
2. According current logic the part of response got read to let you access to headers without any else async operation.
3. From here response awaits (literaly) for you next instructions: will you read the payload or not.
4. If you don't, connection still keeps alive due to TCP and actually, I not sure if some part of payload didn't get buffered on some low level meanwhile. Because server doesn't know what are you doing, it keep sending the data until your socket recvbuf won't be full.

Why should half-readed response be closed by aiohttp automagically and how? Only by some sort of read-timeout, but hey, if you don't need a response payload, just call `.release()` explicitly and cary on. If you read a streamed payload and in the middle decided that the rest part of it is not interested to you, do the same. 

Imagine that you're doing to requests, but reading a files. You opened a file, read a part and didn't explicitly closed. And another one. And again. What happens? Your open fd's counting until hit the limit. The only way to save yourself here from crash is close the files while you don't need them anymore. With the responses there is the same story, expect you can release them to return connection back to the pool.

So, assume reponse as a async file. Should you explicitly release/close it when you don't need it? Yes. Should aiohttp implement own GC to cleanup after you? No. For a single line of code you forgot to write it's too epic task.

My question more pertains to the fact that the frontend and traditional
traitlets config files live in different locations and have significantly
different purposes and symantics (the frontend config can be written by the
live app, but only in the user area). I think we should aim to keep the
command line APIs the same, but don't know how to solve these problems
without:

1) breaking the location and/or content of existing config files.
2) Creating an even uglier implementation or model.

To address Nick's point - our answer to this point is that installation and
activation should always be separate. I know that people (including
ourselves) have figured out how to break that and automatically enable
things like ipywidgets when they are installed. I think we will regret that
in the long run for exactly the reasons you are stating. We should make
enabling extensions easier though - preferably through a nice UI.

> We do have some of this functionality, just not in json config. *Python*
> config files do support modifications for container traits, which json
> config files do not. You can do:
>
> c.Class.dict_trait.update({'a': 5})
>
> and it will add values, rather than overriding the whole trait. We could

> values.

Regarding:

> They don't appear to override the cli options.

The command line is parsed here https://github.com/codecov/codecov-python/blob/master/codecov/__init__.py#L201

``` python
basics.add_argument('--env', '-e', nargs="*", default=os.getenv("CODECOV_ENV"), help="Store environment variables to help distinguish CI builds. Example: http://bit.ly/1ElohCu")
```

Assigned here https://github.com/codecov/codecov-python/blob/master/codecov/__init__.py#L238

``` python
include_env = set(codecov.env or [])
```

At this point ``-e`` or ``-e FOO`` would give ``include_env =set( [])`` or ``include_env = set(['FOO'])``.

Then, on the TravisCI branch this code is called https://github.com/codecov/codecov-python/blob/master/codecov/__init__.py#L286

``` python
            include_env.add('TRAVIS_OS_NAME')
            if language:
                include_env.add('TRAVIS_%s_NAME' % language.upper())

            if language == 'python' and os.getenv('TOXENV'):
                include_env.add('TOXENV')
```

At this point if ``-e`` or ``-e FOO`` was specified rather than ``include_env =set( [])`` or ``include_env = set(['FOO'])``, we get ``include_env = set(['TRAVIS_OS_NAME', 'TRAVIS_PYTHON_NAME', 'TOXENV')])`` or ``include_env = set(['FOO', 'TRAVIS_OS_NAME', 'TRAVIS_PYTHON_NAME', 'TOXENV')])`` (assuming Python).

i.e. The user specified list has been overridden.

Queried headers are returned in [descending order](https://github.com/NSLS-II/metadatastore/blob/f107e0527070548bd6cea3d9b90f91d205478ee9/metadatastore/core.py#L892-L893)

Last headers (mds.find_last) are returned in [descending order](https://github.com/NSLS-II/metadatastore/blob/f107e0527070548bd6cea3d9b90f91d205478ee9/metadatastore/core.py#L892-L893)

Whatever we get from mongo seems in descending order with regards to headers. In other words, mongo driver will provide an ascending list: ``db[-1] == db()[0]`` will return `True`

To me, this is fairly intuitive since most database drivers return descendingly ordered data in this fashion. The most recent data is the first element of the list (since you want to access to the latest data first, ordering it in this fashion makes sense).

The difference between if..else and try..except/for..else seems to have to do with the internal structures: In the IfelseblockNode the endlnode with indentation is attached to the end of the IfNode within the IfelseblockNode, i.e. it is an additional level down, whereas in the ForNode the indentation is attached to the else attribute of the ForNode itself.

In any case, I have discovered a workaround: Manually correct the indentation before appending using the decrease_indentation() method, e.g.
```
from redbaron import RedBaron
code1=RedBaron("test()\n")
code2=RedBaron("def foo():\n    if a:\n        a()\n    else:\n        b()\n")
deindent=len(code2[0].value[0].indentation)-len(code1[0].indentation)
toappend=code2[0].value.copy()
toappend.decrease_indentation(deindent)

# toappend[0] is an endlnode, which doesn't do us much good
code1.append(toappend[1])

print(code1.dumps())
```
This version gives the desired output:
```
test()
if a:
    a()
else:
    b()
```

Here's a bit of a summary for future reference.  Probably belongs somewhere else, but copy/paste isn't that hard.

What it takes to add a new reactor:
* In `pytest_twisted.py`
  * Write an `init_foo_reactor()` function
  * Add `'foo': init_foo_reactor,` to `_reactor_fixtures` where the key will be the string to be passed such as `--reactor=foo`.
* In `testing/test_basic.py`
  * Add `test_blockon_in_hook_with_foo()` decorated by `@skip_if_reactor_not('foo')`
  * Add `test_wrong_reactor_with_foo()` decorated by `@skip_if_reactor_not('foo')`
* In `tox.ini`
  * Adjust `envlist` to include the `fooreactor` factor for the appropriate versions of Python
  * Add conditional `deps` for the new reactor such as `foo: foobar` to the appropriate test environments
  * Add the conditional assignment `foo: reactor_option=foo` to `setenv` in the appropriate test environments
* In `.travis.yml`
  * Consider any extra system packages which may be required

It would be nice to maybe parameterize the tests somehow to avoid some repetition.  At least for the non-default reactors.  It would also be nice to adjust the Tox test environment setup to have a single entry with [complex factor conditions](http://tox.readthedocs.io/en/latest/config.html#complex-factor-conditions), but my first attempts failed.

OK, I've looked into this some more, and it's actually a bit complicated to fix, because doing it right may involve fairly dramatic changes to the way these things are parsed. The crux of the issue is that dots are both a valid separator (`Jan.5.2014` or `01.05.2014`) and the delimiter for decimal fractions (`12:;36:21.44`).

One problem is that it's not possible to distinguish between interpreting `'Jan. 5.2014'` as "January 5, 2014" and "4:50 AM on January 5" (e.g. January 5th + 0.2014 days). Neither format seems particularly likely to be prevalent.

> it will also get a weight of 0 which will cause both stacks to immediately get 50% traffic each which might not be desired.

This (2 stacks with the same weight will receive the same amount of requests no matter what it is) is a result of AWS Route 53 behavior we cannot control (unless we redo the entire routing approach in the STUPS ecosystem). Stacks are created with 0 weight since in a "normal" situation it will mean it will not get any traffic.

I also believe we can't change the behavior of increasing the traffic to also touch the other stack at 0% because until now people could always rely on the fact that stacks with 0% of traffic wouldn't get traffic by accident except on the specific corner case where everything has weight 0.

Do you have any suggestion to improve the behavior in a way that will not break deployment setups that are currently working?

In addition to what Andrew mentioned. As a bit of a side-project, I started experimenting with how this might look in the special case of a TimeSeries on a branch but it's experimental at this point and I think the final solution will likely look different and will use some more of the general infrastructure that Andrew mentioned. I.e., I would not recommend to rely on what I'm describing next as it is experimental and will very likely change. I just wanted to mention it in case it helps you need something quick-and-dirty for some time-critical task. 

If you want, you can have a look at the class [TimeSeriesHelper](https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/compare/enh/select_timeseries#diff-124606c433364b5e158f4f39f610a8e1R9) on the branch ``enh/select_timeseries``. The class just wraps an existing ``TimeSeries`` to provide additional functions. In particular, have a look at the function [subset_series](https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/compare/enh/select_timeseries#diff-124606c433364b5e158f4f39f610a8e1R104) which can take a ``time_range`` and/or an ``index_select`` (e.g., ``numpy.s_[:,1:10]``) to select data from the ``TimeSeries`` and creates a new ``TimeSeries`` for the corresponding data subset. I have not tested it extensively, but it should in principle work with any ``TimeSeries``, i.e., ``subset_series`` should create a new series of the same derived type as the input ``TimeSeries`` and all fields should be copied over. Here is a simple [test case](https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/compare/enh/select_timeseries#diff-58164adb06168536cb3f30be51a1a68fR9) which shows how it should work.  The [TimeSeriesHelper](https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/compare/enh/select_timeseries#diff-124606c433364b5e158f4f39f610a8e1R9) class also has a number of other experimental functions to deal with the timestamps issued, e.g., to convert between stamps and index or to convert the timestamps (which are relative to the session_start_time) into absolute timestamps. There are also some functions to convert a TimeSeries to an [xarray](https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/compare/enh/select_timeseries#diff-124606c433364b5e158f4f39f610a8e1R183) or [pandas series](https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/compare/enh/select_timeseries#diff-124606c433364b5e158f4f39f610a8e1R219)

`CFGAccurate` may fail to resolve some indirect jumps, and consequently misses all blocks that are only reachable when those jump targets are taken.
I install z3 manually, then can I remove the Z3 requirement and continue installation?

- Currently `CFGAccurate` does not have full support for resolving jump tables. You should be able to see call edges from `callsites()` to four functions after we finish the jumptable resolving support for `CFGAccurate`.

- As @rhelmot said, `CFGFast` works by looking for known function epilogues as well as using symbols from the binary, and that's why `CFGFast` sees all functions. However, `CFGFast` does not seem to be able to resolve the jump table either, and as a result, you don't see call edges from `callsites()` to any of those four functions in `CFGFast`.

- With `symbolic_back_traversal=True` or `advanced_back_slicing=True`, `CFGAccurate` should still be able to find those call edges from `callsites()` to those four functions. However, as you can see from your code, `i` can be any value from `MIN_INT` to `MAX_INT`, which could be the root cause of the warning:
> Exit state has over 257 possible solutions. Likely unconstrained; skipping. <BV64 reg_18_47_64>
I believe `CFGAccurate` is ignoring all resolved results for an indirect jump if there are more than 257 possible solutions, which is exactly the case in your example. You can try to limit the range of `i` (e.g. add a check in your code snippet to make sure `i` is less than 100 and greater than or equal to 0) and rebuild the CFG on the new program.

And also instances of the transit time well outside the rise/set times. Repro code below. I attached my current TLE file so we're using the same data. I did a plot (attached) of duration vs transit elevation of a week's worth of calculations and you can see there are a number of high passes with short duration. The negative elevations are caused by the transit time be much later than the rise/set. I have a script that does all the passes for all the Iridium satellites for a week, if that would help. [iridium-tle.txt](https://github.com/pytroll/pyorbital/files/1791171/iridium-tle.txt) ``` from pyorbital import tlefile from pyorbital.orbital import Orbital from datetime import datetime TLE_FILE_NAME = 'iridium-tle.txt' HORIZON = 40 # altitude (km) above seal-level and lat lon of the observer ALTITUDE_ASL = 0.5 LON = 170.556 LAT = -43.368 #tlefile.TLE_URLS = ('https://celestrak.com/NORAD/elements/iridium.txt', ) #tlefile.fetch(TLE_FILE_NAME) def do_calc(d, sat_name): orb = Orbital(sat_name, TLE_FILE_NAME) passes = orb.get_next_passes(d, 1, LON, LAT, ALTITUDE_ASL, horizon=HORIZON) rise_az, rise_el = orb.get_observer_look(passes[0][0], LON, LAT, ALTITUDE_ASL) transit_az, transit_el = orb.get_observer_look(passes[0][2], LON, LAT, ALTITUDE_ASL) set_az, set_el = orb.get_observer_look(passes[0][1], LON, LAT, ALTITUDE_ASL) print(sat_name) print(passes[0][0], rise_az, rise_el) print(passes[0][2], transit_az, transit_el) print(passes[0][1], set_az, set_el) print() do_calc(datetime(2018, 3, 9, 17, 35, 0), 'IRIDIUM 40 [-]') do_calc(datetime(2018, 3, 8, 23, 21, 0), 'IRIDIUM 47 [+]') ```

The problem of FFTW_MEASURE is that it can be very slow on the first run compared to, say, numpy's fftpack.  Of course this is the whole point of FFTW, but this makes the interfaces API more problematic to use as a drop-in replacement of numpy.fft and scipy.fftpack.  So yes I'd favor switching to FFTW_ESTIMATE as default.

The proposed global defaults, likewise, are there to help using the interfaces as a global drop-in replacement.  I think they'd do more good than harm (in fact the only problem I can see is an increase or decrease in performance if someone else messes with the globals, and it should at least be easy to find the bottleneck), but I do understand your point about the risks.

I don't completely understand the details of your use case, but if you need to call a Python function with an `Acknowledgment` object, that function should have access to a Python `Acknowledgment` type.

Let's maybe make this more concrete:
Suppose you have
```
def foo(ack):
   print type(ack)
   return 42
```
and you call that function as before
```
// Import the Python module so we can call foo...
Acknowledgment acknowledgment;
foo(pybind11::cast(acknowledgment));
```

What would you expect that thing to print? The moment you call `pybind11::cast` that object 'lives in the Python interpreter' and needs to have a Python type, etc. Constructing `py::class_<Acknowledgment>(module, "PythonAcknowledgment")` before does that: it does not just 'define the Python interface', but creates a new Python class type `PythonAcknowledgment` and registers the mapping between the C++ `Acknowledgment` and Python `PythonAcknowledgment`.

I suspect (I haven't downloaded and unpacked all the packages to check) that that PPA maintainer has de-vendored urllib3, as is the Debian way. In a typical certbot install, it is vendored inside `requests`. If we apply this patch, we will crash on typical installs, where there is no top-level urllib3 lib: notice it is not in https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces/letsencrypt-auto-requirements.txt.

The hardline answer is that the PPA maintainer is responsible for applying and maintaining this patch, since he's the one changing the import paths. If we wanted to be nice, at the expense of adding a branch to our code, we could catch an ImportError and try for a top-level urllib3 as a fallback. I lean slightly toward the former, because monkeypatching is scary enough without adding ifs around it. (What if somebody had a non-pinned install and a different version of requests, in which the vendored path was different? We would fall back to a top-level urllib3, which, if installed, could conceivably interact badly with the requests-vendored version if its monkeypatcher got called as well.)

I'm glad we were able to fix this problem for you, however, this patch hasn't been included in a release yet. It will in our 0.20.0 release at the beginning of next month though.

As for updating `certbot-auto`, I'm sorry for the confusion. We have an issue for a `--install-only` flag at #4741 here which as I've described it, would also update you to the latest version and stop if you already have Certbot installed. I'll make a note about this there to make sure it's clear that flag can be used for both purposes.

Another user encountered this problem with a directive like the following in a virtual host:
```
ServerName ${FOO}.example.org
```

> I don't think I fully understand why is this failing (and how does it depend on versions of everything)

I think what's going on is cryptography didn't support making certs with custom extensions until cryptography 1.3. This isn't documented in their changelog, but this seems to be suggested by https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/issues/2747.

I'd like to avoid bumping our cryptography requirement higher than 1.2.3 though. This is the version in Ubuntu Xenial and we've been working to get a new version of Certbot shipped there for a long time now. Getting an update to a crypto library used by many other projects is a much harder sell than just updating Certbot itself.

> Anyway, how should I implement your proposal to fail alpn on old pyopenssl? Should I just make sure everything that worked before still works, and possibly raise an exception in the new stuff?

Pretty much. I haven't dug into it so it might already work this way, but we shouldn't raise an exception if the CA includes a TLS-SNI-01 challenge in the authorization object regardless of the version of our dependencies. Instead, I'd like us to only raise an exception when trying to perform/verify the challenge.

> What about tests? Make alpn tests conditional on version, and simply do not run them on older?

That works. If you're feeling ambitious, you could also add tests that mock out the libraries so the code is still tested when run with the older libraries. I don't think that's totally necessary though.

I'm personally not sure if that alone will work. Depending on how long ago the user encountered the error in this PR, `should_autodeploy` could return `True` or `False`. My understanding is the default `deploy_before_expiry` value is 5 days. You could potentially fix this problem by changing the default value to be the same as `renew_before_expiry`.

Also, I could be wrong, but I think other work would have to be done to actually respect `deploy_before_expiry`. For instance, as we just discussed, we choose whether or not to renew on the basis of `should_autorenew`. During renewal we do [this](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/renewal.py#L249) which immediately deploys the cert, regardless of the `deploy_before_expiry` value.
